annotations.label: Subtraction

anchor.snippet: Subtraction is an operation that represents removal of objects from a collection

annotations.note: Subtraction is provided by the `Sub` typeclass and structured via `SubNegMonoid`/`SubtractionMonoid` in `Mathlib.Algebra.Group.Defs`.

annotations.label: Subtraction as inverse of addition

anchor.snippet: subtraction is the inverse of addition

annotations.note: `SubNegMonoid.sub_eq_add_neg` (and the unfolded `sub_eq_add_neg`) state `a - b = a + -b`, formalizing subtraction as the additive-inverse operation.

annotations.label: Anti-commutativity (lead)

anchor.snippet: Subtraction is anti-commutative, meaning that changing the order changes the sign of the answer

annotations.note: `neg_sub : -(a - b) = b - a` in `Mathlib.Algebra.Group.Basic` captures anti-commutativity of subtraction.

annotations.label: Non-associativity (lead)

anchor.snippet: It is also not associative , meaning that when one subtracts more than two numbers, the order in which subtraction is performed matters

annotations.note: Mathlib has lemmas like `sub_sub` rewriting iterated subtractions, but does not state non-associativity as a theorem.

annotations.label: Subtraction by zero

anchor.snippet: Because 0 is the additive identity , subtraction of it does not change a number

annotations.note: `sub_zero : a - 0 = a` is a standard lemma in `Mathlib.Algebra.Group.Basic`.

annotations.label: Minuend, subtrahend, difference

anchor.snippet: The number being subtracted is the subtrahend , while the number it is subtracted from is the minuend

annotations.note: These are purely linguistic terms for arguments of subtraction and have no corresponding Mathlib declaration.

anchors.snippet: The number being subtracted is the subtrahend , while the number it is subtracted from is the minuend

annotations.label: Line segment subtraction model

anchor.snippet: Imagine a line segment of length b with the left end labeled a and the right end labeled c

annotations.note: An informal geometric illustration with no specific Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Subtraction on integer line

anchor.snippet: it takes 4 steps to the left from 3 to get to

annotations.note: A specific numeric illustration not formalized as a named decl in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Non-closure of natural number subtraction

anchor.snippet: Subtraction of natural numbers is not closed

annotations.note: Mathlib uses the truncated `Nat.sub` (with `Nat.sub_eq_zero_iff_le` capturing saturation) rather than directly stating non-closure.

annotations.label: Subtraction of real numbers

anchor.snippet: The subtraction of a real number (the subtrahend) from another (the minuend) can then be defined as the addition of the minuend and the additive inverse of the subtrahend

annotations.note: `Real` inherits `SubNegMonoid`, so `sub_eq_add_neg` gives the stated definition on ℝ.

annotations.label: Anti-commutativity

anchor.snippet: Subtraction is anti-commutative , meaning that if one reverses the terms in a difference left-to-right, the result is the negative of the original result

annotations.note: `neg_sub : -(a - b) = b - a` directly states anti-commutativity.

annotations.label: Non-associativity

anchor.snippet: Subtraction is non-associative , which comes up when one tries to define repeated subtraction

annotations.note: Mathlib provides `sub_sub` rewrites but does not formalize the meta-statement that subtraction is non-associative.

annotations.label: Predecessor

anchor.snippet: the integer ( a − 1) is the largest integer less than a , also known as the predecessor of a

annotations.note: `PredOrder` and `Order.pred` in `Mathlib.Order.SuccPred.Basic` formalize the predecessor in any preorder, and on ℤ this is `a - 1`.

annotations.label: Units in subtraction

anchor.snippet: When subtracting two numbers with units of measurement such as kilograms or pounds , they must have the same unit

annotations.note: Mathlib does not model physical units of measurement.

annotations.label: Percentage change vs. percentage point change

anchor.snippet: Percentage change represents the relative change between the two quantities as a percentage, while percentage point change is simply the number obtained by subtracting the two percentages

annotations.note: Percentage change is not a defined notion in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Defective widgets percentage

anchor.snippet: suppose that 30% of widgets made in a factory are defective

annotations.note: A pedagogical numerical example with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Method of complements

anchor.snippet: The method of complements is a technique used to subtract one number from another using only the addition of positive numbers

annotations.note: The method of complements as an arithmetic technique is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Binary subtraction via complements

anchor.snippet: the ones' complement of y is added to x and one is added to the sum

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize ones'/twos' complement binary subtraction algorithms.

annotations.label: American method worked example (704 − 512)

anchor.snippet: The minuend is 704, the subtrahend is 512

annotations.note: A pedagogical worked example not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Austrian method example

anchor.snippet: The required sum (5) is too small

annotations.note: A pedagogical example of an arithmetic method not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Subtraction from left to right example

anchor.snippet: This result is only penciled in

annotations.note: A pedagogical algorithm example with no Mathlib analogue.

annotations.label: American method (borrowing)

anchor.snippet: each digit of the subtrahend is subtracted from the digit above it starting from right to left

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize the elementary borrowing algorithm for decimal subtraction.

annotations.label: Trade first method

anchor.snippet: A variant of the American method where all borrowing is done before all subtraction

annotations.note: This pedagogical variant is not represented in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Partial differences method

anchor.snippet: The partial differences method is different from other vertical subtraction methods because no borrowing or carrying takes place

annotations.note: Not a formalized algorithm in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Counting up example

anchor.snippet: one can count up the numbers between the subtrahend and the minuend

annotations.note: An informal counting strategy with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Breaking up the subtraction example

anchor.snippet: to split up the subtraction into small steps

annotations.note: A pedagogical strategy not represented as a Mathlib decl.

annotations.label: Same change method

anchor.snippet: The same change method uses the fact that adding or subtracting the same number from the minuend and subtrahend does not change the answer

annotations.note: The underlying identity `(a + c) - (b + c) = a - b` follows from lemmas like `sub_add_sub_cancel`/`add_sub_add_right_eq_sub`, but the method itself is not formalized.
